merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations and the American Legation in London were established when John Adams presented his credentials as Minister Plenipotentiary to King George III.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations and the American Legation in London were established on June 1, 1785, when John Adams presented his credentials as Minister Plenipotentiary to King George III.

merged_events.event_description: John Adams closed the legation in 1788 due to frustration with the cool reception, leaving the post vacant for four years.

evidence.quote: Adams, however, became so frustrated with the cool reception that he closed the legation in 1788 and the post remained vacant for four years.

merged_events.event_description: The American Legation in London was re-opened after four years of vacancy following Adams's closure in 1788.

evidence.quote: Adams, however, became so frustrated with the cool reception that he closed the legation in 1788 and the post remained vacant for four years.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. legation in London was closed by Chargé d'Affaires Jonathan Russell following the U.S. declaration of war against the United Kingdom on June 18, 1812.

evidence.quote: The United States broke relations with the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland when it declared war against its former colonial ruler on June 18, 1812, although Chargé d'Affaires Jonathan Russell did not close the legation until July 29, 1812.

merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations were re-established when John Quincy Adams presented his credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary in London.

evidence.quote: Future President John Quincy Adams, son of John Adams, was appointed Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, to the United Kingdom on February 28, 1815, and presented his credentials in London on August 8, 1815.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Legation in London was elevated to an Embassy when Ambassador Thomas Bayard presented his credentials to the Court of St. James.

evidence.quote: The U.S. legation in London became the U.S. Embassy in London when Ambassador Thomas Bayard presented his credentials to the Court of St. James on June 22, 1893.

merged_events.event_description: The United States declared war on Great Britain; Chargé d'Affaires Jonathan Russell suspended his official functions, ending U.S. diplomatic representation.

evidence.quote: The United States declared war on Great Britain on 1812-06-18. Note: Commission (issued during a recess of the Senate) not of record, but enclosed with an instruction of July 27, 1811. Russell received unofficial notice of the U.S. declaration of war on July 29, 1812, and suspended his official functions.

merged_events.event_description: John Quincy Adams presented credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, re-establishing the U.S. legation after the War of 1812.

evidence.quote: John Quincy Adams, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1815-02-28. Credentials presented: 1815-06-08. Presented recall on 1817-05-14. Note: Commissioned to the court of the Prince Regent of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.

matched.notes: Exact match on date (1785-06-01) and status (Legation). Both reference John Adams presenting credentials.

RDCR gives only the year 1788 without a specific date. CSV date of 1788-02-20 is noted as from Chiefs of Mission page (POCOM). Since RDCR lacks a specific institutional date, the POCOM-derived date is appropriate per source priority rules.

RDCR only implies ~1792 from 'four years' after 1788 closure (low confidence extraction). CSV date of 1792-08-09 is noted as from Chiefs of Mission page (POCOM). Since RDCR lacks a specific date, the POCOM-derived date is appropriate per source priority rules.

RDCR (Event 3) explicitly states the legation was not closed until July 29, 1812, matching the CSV. POCOM (Event 6) gives 1812-06-18 (war declaration date), but RDCR provides the explicit institutional closure date, so RDCR wins per source priority. No discrepancy — CSV correctly follows RDCR.

RDCR (Event 4) explicitly states credentials were presented on August 8, 1815, matching the CSV. POCOM (Event 7) gives 1815-06-08, which likely reflects a transcription or data error in the extraction (June vs August). RDCR provides an explicit institutional date ('presented his credentials in London on August 8, 1815'), so RDCR wins. CSV correctly follows RDCR.
Exact match on date (1893-06-22) and status (Embassy). Both reference Ambassador Thomas Bayard presenting credentials at the Court of St. James.
